summary: Arqel dogfood loop — Round 4: re-detect against main (28 bugs resolved rounds 0-3). Deep probes into less-covered areas (audit/realtime/mcp/cli/export-xlsx/command-palette/relation+form edge cases). Seen-filtered (28 sigs), adversarial verify. Stop after 2 consecutive clean rounds.
logs: Round 4 detection: 11 from clusters = 11 after dedup/seen-filter
Round 4 verify: 7 confirmed NEW framework bugs, 1 app-misuse, 1 not-a-bug

confirmedFrameworkBugs.title: VersionRestoreController skips authorization entirely when the model uses a Laravel Policy (Gate::has('update') never sees policies)
confirmedFrameworkBugs.fixSketch: Replace the `if (Gate::has('update')) { Gate::authorize('update', $record); }` guard with the canonical core pattern: skip only when there is neither a named gate nor a policy, otherwise enforce. E.g. `if (Gate::has('update') || Gate::getPolicyFor($record::class)) { Gate::authorize('update', $record); }` (mirrors core's ResourceController::authorize() at lines 351-358).
confirmedFrameworkBugs.reasoning: Reproduced live against packages/versioning/src on current main. VersionRestoreController.php:52 guards authorization with `if (Gate::has('update')) { Gate::authorize('update', $record); }`. Confirmed in the vendored Laravel Gate.php:123-134 that has() only consults $this->abilities (populated by Gate::define), never policies. I wrote a Feature test registering a denying Policy via Gate::policy(Article::class, DenyAllArticlePolicy) (no named gate). Assertions inside the test confirmed Gate::has('update')===false AND Gate::denies('update', new Article)===true, yet the restore endpoint returned HTTP 200 and mutated the record (restore succeeded) instead of 403 — the policy was bypassed. This is strictly weaker than the project's own canonical pattern in core ResourceController::authorize() (lines 351-358), which falls back to Gate::getPolicyFor($modelClass) and only short-circuits when neither a named gate nor a policy exists. The existing test VersionRestoreControllerTest.php:124 only exercises the named-gate path (Gate::define('update', ...)), giving false confidence and never covering the Gate::policy() path that idiomatic Laravel apps use (CLAUDE.md rule #4 mandates Laravel-native Policies). Impact: any authenticated user can restore any record (and reach historical payloads via the Versionable trait) on models protected by a Policy. Severity high rather than blocker because it requires the app to authorize via a Policy (not a named gate) and requires authentication; not a deferred/documented feature.
confirmedFrameworkBugs.evidence: packages/versioning/src/Http/Controllers/VersionRestoreController.php:52 guards restore with `if (Gate::has('update')) { Gate::authorize('update', $record); }`. Laravel's Gate::has() (apps/showcase/vendor/laravel/framework/src/Illuminate/Auth/Access/Gate.php:123) only checks $this->abilities (registered via Gate::define) and NEVER consults policies. Verified live in showcase: with `Gate::policy(Post::class, PolicyThatDeniesUpdate)`, `Gate::has('update')` returns false, so the guard is skipped and the restore proceeds (returns 200, mutates the record) even though the policy denies. CLAUDE.md rule #4 mandates Laravel-native Policies, and core's own ResourceController.php:94 correctly uses `$this->authorize('update', $record)`. The test VersionRestoreControllerTest.php:124 only asserts the 403 path via `Gate::define('update', ...)` (a named gate), giving false confidence; it never tests the Gate::policy() path that real apps use. Impact: any authenticated user can roll back any record to any prior version (and via the same trait reach historical PII in payloads) regardless of the model's update Policy.

confirmedFrameworkBugs.title: VersionHistoryController skips the view authorization check when the model uses a Policy, exposing full snapshot payloads (PII/secrets)
confirmedFrameworkBugs.fixSketch: Replace the `Gate::has('view')`-gated check with an unconditional policy-aware authorization that only runs when a policy exists, e.g. `if (Gate::getPolicyFor($model) !== null) { Gate::forUser($request->user())->authorize('view', $model); }` (or simply `Gate::authorize('view', $model)` and let core register a default). Apply the identical fix to VersionRestoreController.php:52 which gates `update` the same broken way via `Gate::has('update')`.
confirmedFrameworkBugs.reasoning: Reproduced live in the versioning package's own testbench. Laravel's Gate::has($ability) (vendor .../Auth/Access/Gate.php:123) only inspects $this->abilities — the array populated by Gate::define(). Policies registered via Gate::policy() live in a separate $this->policies map and are resolved through getPolicyFor() inside resolveAuthCallback (line 627). Therefore Gate::has('view') is FALSE for any model whose 'view' authorization comes from a Policy class, and the controller guard `if (Gate::has('view') && ! Gate::allows('view', $model))` short-circuits, skipping the check entirely. My repro registered a DenyAllArticlePolicy (view() => false): Gate::forUser($u)->allows('view',$article) returned false (policy denies) while Gate::has('view') returned false, and the HTTP request returned 200 instead of 403, with ?include=payload exposing the full snapshot (title=Secret, body=leak) that VersionPresenter's own docblock warns 'pode conter PII e segredos do model'. The route (routes/web.php:19) only applies web+auth middleware — no can: middleware — so the controller is the sole authorization boundary. The class docblock (lines 26-28) explicitly promises that a registered policy IS honored, so this is the implementation contradicting its documented contract, not intended behavior; it is not a deferred feature. Contrast core ResourceController::show which correctly uses $this->authorize('view', $record). The sibling VersionRestoreController.php:52 has the identical Gate::has('update') flaw, making this a systemic pattern in the package. Not a blocker because it requires an authenticated user, a policy-only (no Gate::define) app, and the package route mounted; the worst-case payload disclosure needs ?include=payload, though the default listing still leaks change summaries/diffs.
confirmedFrameworkBugs.evidence: packages/versioning/src/Http/Controllers/VersionHistoryController.php:73 guards with `if (Gate::has('view') && ! Gate::allows('view', $model))`. Because Gate::has('view') is false for any model relying on a Policy (verified live: `Gate::has('view')` = false with Gate::policy registered, while the policy's view() would have denied), the whole condition short-circuits and the request returns 200. With `?include=payload` the controller returns the complete `$model->getAttributes()` snapshot for every version — the VersionPresenter docblock itself warns the payload 'pode conter PII e segredos do model'. Contrast core's ResourceController.php:84 `$this->authorize('view', $record)`. No test covers the Gate::policy() path. Impact: authorization bypass + sensitive data disclosure for policy-protected models.

confirmedFrameworkBugs.title: Collab AwarenessChannelAuthorizer falls through to allow-all when the model uses a Policy (Gate::has('view') misses policies), leaking Yjs cursor/presence channels
confirmedFrameworkBugs.fixSketch: Detect policy registration too: `if (Gate::has('view') || Gate::getPolicyFor($record) !== null) { return Gate::forUser($user)->check('view', $record); } return true;` so policy-protected records are authorized via the policy instead of falling through to allow-all.
confirmedFrameworkBugs.reasoning: Reproduced concretely in packages/realtime with a Pest test. Laravel's `Gate::has($ability)` (vendor Gate.php:123-134) only inspects `$this->abilities` populated by `Gate::define()`; policy registrations live in a separate `$this->policies` map. So when a model is protected only by `Gate::policy(Post::class, SomePolicy::class)`, `Gate::has('view')` returns false. AwarenessChannelAuthorizer.php:51 therefore skips the `Gate::forUser($user)->check('view', $record)` branch and falls through to `return true` (line 55), authorizing the private channel `arqel.collab.{modelType}.{modelId}.{field}` for a user the policy explicitly denies. My repro proved both: `Gate::has('view')===false` under a policy, and `Gate::forUser($user)->check('view',$post)===false` (policy denies) while `authorize(...)===true` (channel allows). This channel carries the live Yjs document `state` (the actual field content, broadcast by Events\YjsUpdateReceived as `collab.update`) plus cursor/presence — so it is a cross-record/cross-tenant content leak, not merely metadata. This is framework-side, not app-misuse: CLAUDE.md rule #4 mandates Laravel-native Policies as the canonical authorization mechanism, and the SKILL.md states the authorizer should "honor Gate view when defined"; a policy IS the definition of view authorization, so honoring only Gate::define is an incomplete authorization path, not a documented/deferred feature. The existing test (AwarenessChannelAuthorizerTest.php:56) only exercises the Gate::define deny path, so the gap is invisible to the suite. Fix is small and uses Laravel's own public API (Gate::getPolicyFor, confirmed public at Gate.php:659).
confirmedFrameworkBugs.evidence: packages/realtime/src/Collab/AwarenessChannelAuthorizer.php:51 does `if (Gate::has('view')) { return Gate::forUser($user)->check('view', $record); } return true;`. Verified live in showcase: with `Gate::policy(Post::class, PolicyThatDeniesView)`, `Gate::has('view')` returns false, so the authorizer returns true (allow) for a user the policy explicitly denies. This authorizes the `arqel.collab.{modelType}.{modelId}.{field}` private channel (channels.php:51) which carries collaborative-editing presence and cursor positions for a specific record — a cross-record / cross-tenant leak vector when apps protect records with Policies (Laravel-native, per CLAUDE.md #4). The test AwarenessChannelAuthorizerTest.php:56 only asserts the deny path via `Gate::define('view', ...)`, never via Gate::policy(), so the suite stays green.

confirmedFrameworkBugs.title: Dashboard-level canSee()/canBeSeenBy() authorization is never enforced — restricted dashboards leak to any authenticated user
confirmedFrameworkBugs.rootCauseFile: packages/widgets/src/Http/Controllers/DashboardController.php:31-42 (and WidgetDataController.php:39-51); the dead gate is Dashboard::canBeSeenBy at packages/widgets/src/Dashboard.php:212
confirmedFrameworkBugs.fixSketch: In DashboardController::show, after resolving the dashboard add `abort_unless($dashboard->canBeSeenBy($request->user()), 403);` before resolve(); mirror the same dashboard-level check in WidgetDataController::show right after `$dashboard = $registry->get($dashboardId)` (in addition to the existing per-widget check). Add a feature test asserting a canSee(fn()=>false) dashboard returns 403.
confirmedFrameworkBugs.reasoning: Reproduced concretely. Dashboard::canSee()/canBeSeenBy() (Dashboard.php:205-219) is a fully implemented, public, unit-tested authorization gate, the exact parallel of Widget::canSee()/canBeSeenBy() which IS enforced (WidgetDataController.php:51 and Dashboard::resolve():278). grep confirms Dashboard::canBeSeenBy has ZERO call sites outside its own definition. DashboardController::show only does abort_if(null,404) then resolve(); resolve() filters only per-WIDGET canBeSeenBy, never the dashboard-level gate (despite findWidget's own comment at Dashboard.php:344 stating authorization is expected to be enforced by callers). routes/admin.php applies only ['web','auth']. Live repro using the real package classes: a Dashboard::make('secret')->canSee(fn()=>false)->widgets([revenue widget value=999999]) yields canBeSeenBy(null)===false yet resolve(null) returns widgets=[{data:{value:999999}}], which the controller passes to Inertia with HTTP 200 — leaking a restricted dashboard to any authenticated user. This is broken access control / a dead security method, not a deferred feature: no spec/ADR marks dashboard-level auth as out of scope, the API is shipped in the current package, and the parallel widget gate is enforced, establishing clear intent. Could not refute. Severity high rather than blocker: requires the developer to opt in via canSee() and the auth middleware still requires authentication, so it is an authenticated-user privilege leak, not an unauthenticated one.
confirmedFrameworkBugs.evidence: Dashboard.php defines canSee()/canBeSeenBy(?Authenticatable) (lines 205-219, unit-tested at tests/Unit/DashboardTest.php:130), but NEITHER controller ever calls it. DashboardController::show (packages/widgets/src/Http/Controllers/DashboardController.php:31-43) only does abort_if($dashboard===null,404) then resolve(); WidgetDataController::show (lines 39-62) checks only $widget->canBeSeenBy() not $dashboard->canBeSeenBy(). routes/admin.php applies only ['web','auth']. grep confirms Dashboard::canBeSeenBy has zero call sites outside its own definition. Live repro in apps/showcase: a Dashboard::make('secret',...)->canSee(fn()=>false)->widgets([StatWidget::make('revenue')->value(999999)]) returns HTTP 200 from DashboardController::show and leaks {widgets:[{data:{value:999999}}]} even though $dash->canBeSeenBy(null)===false. A developer who restricts a dashboard via canSee() gets silently no protection — broken access control / dead security method.

confirmedFrameworkBugs.signature: fields/src/Http/Controllers/FieldSearchController.php:127 + FieldUploadController.php:137 resolveFieldOrFail() uses fields() not effectiveFields()
confirmedFrameworkBugs.title: BelongsTo search + File upload endpoints resolve fields via fields() not effectiveFields(), 404 for form()-only schemas
confirmedFrameworkBugs.fixSketch: In both resolveFieldOrFail() methods, iterate `$resource->effectiveFields()` instead of `$resource->fields()`; the existing `$field instanceof Field` guard already filters out non-Field layout objects returned by form()->getFields().
confirmedFrameworkBugs.reasoning: Reproduced by code inspection on current main. Both FieldSearchController::resolveFieldOrFail (line 127) and FieldUploadController::resolveFieldOrFail (line 137) iterate `$resource->fields()` and abort(404) when the named field isn't present. Meanwhile, since PR #44's fields()/form() unification, Resource::effectiveFields() (Resource.php:169-182) returns the form's flattened getFields() when a form() schema is declared, and BOTH validation (ResourceController::extractRules -> effectiveFields(), line 441) and rendering (InertiaDataBuilder line 314) read effectiveFields(). The HasFields contract (line 29) exposes effectiveFields() as the canonical source. Class hierarchy confirmed: BelongsToField extends Field; ImageField extends FileField extends Field. Therefore a Resource that declares a searchable BelongsToField or a FileField/ImageField ONLY inside its form() layout (Section::make()->schema([...])) and omits it from the abstract fields() list will render and validate correctly but get 404 on GET {resource}/fields/{field}/search and POST/DELETE {resource}/fields/{field}/upload, because resolveFieldOrFail cannot see form()-only fields. This is a genuine consistency bug: the two async endpoints were not migrated to effectiveFields() when PR #44 unified the rest of the framework. Coverage gap confirmed: both fixtures (OwningResource, UploadingResource) declare via fields() only, and grepping the entire fields test suite for form()/effectiveFields/Section::make returned nothing, so the form()-only path through these controllers is completely untested. Not a refutable false positive and not a deferred/intended behavior — it directly contradicts the documented unification intent ('a layout-aware form() does not require re-declaring every field in fields()', Resource.php:158-162). Severity is medium rather than high: it only manifests under the form()-only declaration pattern; the common pattern (fields listed in fields(), as all showcase resources and fixtures do) is unaffected, so async relation-autocomplete and uploads work for the mainstream path. The fix is a one-line swap per controller, made safe by the existing `$field instanceof Field` guard which discards non-Field layout objects.
confirmedFrameworkBugs.evidence: packages/fields/src/Http/Controllers/FieldSearchController.php:127 and packages/fields/src/Http/Controllers/FieldUploadController.php:137 both iterate `$resource->fields()` in resolveFieldOrFail() and abort(404) when the field name isn't found there. Meanwhile validation (ResourceController::extractRules -> Resource::effectiveFields(), core/.../ResourceController.php:441) and rendering both read effectiveFields(), which returns Form::getFields() (flattened from form() layout) when a form() schema is declared (Resource.php:169-182). This is exactly the fields()/form() unification PR #44 introduced. Consequence: a Resource that declares a BelongsToField (or FileField/ImageField) ONLY inside form() layout (e.g. Section::make()->schema([BelongsToField::make(...)->searchable()])) and does NOT also list it in fields() renders and validates correctly, but the async relation-autocomplete GET {resource}/fields/{field}/search and the POST/DELETE {resource}/fields/{field}/upload both return 404 because resolveFieldOrFail can't see form()-only fields. The two controllers should resolve against effectiveFields() (duck-typed) like the rest of the framework. All existing fixtures (OwningResource, UploadingResource) declare via fields(), so the gap is untested: FieldSearchControllerTest/FieldUploadControllerTest have zero form()/effectiveFields coverage (grep returned nothing).

confirmedFrameworkBugs.title: Deferred widget data endpoint drops dashboard-declared filter defaults — refetch payload diverges from SSR
confirmedFrameworkBugs.fixSketch: Before reading data(), seed the dashboard's declared defaults into the widget like resolve() does: merge $dashboard->getFilters() (filterDefaults) under request filters — e.g. $widget->filters(array_merge($dashboard->getFilters(), is_array($filters) ? $filters : [], $widget->getFilters())) so request filters still win but declared defaults are applied when the client sends none.
confirmedFrameworkBugs.reasoning: Reproduced concretely. Dashboard::resolve() (packages/widgets/src/Dashboard.php:285-288) merges $this->filters (= filterDefaults set in declarative filters() mode, Dashboard.php:191) into every widget before data() runs, so the SSR/Inertia path computes widget data WITH declared filter defaults. WidgetDataController::show (lines 53-57) only applies $request->input('filters') and never reads $dashboard->getFilters()/getFilterDefaults(); it returns $widget->data() with $this->filters left empty when the client sends no filters[]. Widget::filterValue() reads $this->filters[$name] (Widget.php:143-146), so the divergence reaches data(). The client cannot mask this: DashboardController::show forwards filterValues = $request->input('filters', []) (empty on first deep-link), ArqelDashboardPage passes filterValues ?? {}, and WidgetRenderer.withFilters only serialises non-empty values — so on a deferred widget's first lazy fetch (data===null from SSR) or any poll where no filter is chosen, the endpoint emits data computed WITHOUT defaults, differing from initial render. A repro Pest test (dashboard with SelectFilter::make('segment')->default('all'); resolve() echoes {segment:all} but the controller with no request filters echoes {}) failed as expected, then was removed. Existing WidgetDataControllerTest never exercises declared-default dashboards through the controller, and DashboardFilterPropagationTest only covers resolve(), so this is an untested gap rather than intended behavior. Not export/EXPORT-007/008. Severity medium: data-consistency, self-healing after a filter interaction, no crash/security impact.
confirmedFrameworkBugs.evidence: Dashboard::resolve() merges $this->filters (dashboard filter defaults from declared Filters) into every widget before data() (packages/widgets/src/Dashboard.php:285-288). But WidgetDataController::show (packages/widgets/src/Http/Controllers/WidgetDataController.php:53-57) only applies $request->input('filters') and NEVER reads $dashboard->getFilterDefaults(); it then returns $widget->data() directly. On a deferred widget's first lazy fetch (before the user changes any DashboardFilters, so the client sends no filters[]), the widget computes with NO dashboard filter defaults applied, producing different data than the SSR/Inertia path which seeded them. Consistency bug between initial render and deferred/poll refetch.

confirmedFrameworkBugs.title: ScopedUnique crashes with QueryException when tenant FK column is absent — documented graceful skip is not implemented
confirmedFrameworkBugs.fixSketch: Before applying the tenant where(), guard with a schema check, e.g. `if ($tenant !== null && $connection->getSchemaBuilder()->hasColumn($this->table, $tenantKey)) { $query->where($tenantKey, $tenant->getKey()); }` (cache hasColumn or wrap exists() in try/catch as a fallback); add a missing-column unit test.
confirmedFrameworkBugs.reasoning:
The docblock (ScopedUnique.php:31-34) explicitly guarantees: "When the configured tenant FK column does not exist on the target table, the tenant filter is skipped (resilient to misconfiguration)." grep confirms "column does not exist" appears ONLY in that comment; there is no hasColumn/getColumnListing/schema check anywhere in packages/tenant/src (confirmed exit:1). validate() unconditionally calls $query->where($tenantKey, $tenant->getKey()) (lines 77-78) whenever a tenant is current and $tenantKey is non-null (default 'tenant_id'), with no try/catch around $query->exists(). So the documented graceful global-unique fallback is genuinely unimplemented, and the missing-column case is untested. CLASSIFICATION = framework-bug: this is an unmet contract inside the framework package, not app misuse of an undocumented edge — the framework itself promises the resilience.

HOWEVER, the evidence's specific reproduction ("produces SQLSTATE column 'tenant_id' not found") does NOT reproduce on the dogfood baseline. Both apps/showcase and apps/tenant-demo use DB_CONNECTION=sqlite. I built a real in-memory SQLite users table with NO tenant_id column, set a current tenant, and ran `new ScopedUnique('users','email')->validate(...)`: NO exception, failed=false. Root cause: Laravel's compileExists emits `... and "tenant_id" = ?` with a DOUBLE-QUOTED identifier, and SQLite's documented double-quoted-string-literal misfeature treats the unknown identifier "tenant_id" as the string literal 'tenant_id' instead of erroring, so exists() returns false — coincidentally delivering exactly the documented global-unique fallback. (An UNquoted raw `tenant_id` reference does throw QueryException on SQLite, but the builder always quotes, so the rule's real path is safe on SQLite.) On MySQL/PostgreSQL (declared in showcase config/database.php) a quoted nonexistent column raises Unknown column / column does not exist, so there the uncaught QueryException is real and the documented skip is absent.

Net: real framework documentation-vs-implementation gap, but (a) it requires misapplying a tenant-scoping rule to a non-tenant table, (b) it cannot crash on the SQLite dogfood baseline, and (c) the rule has zero call sites in any app (only vendor autoload entries). Severity low rather than medium: the crash surface is a developer misconfiguration on non-SQLite drivers, not a default-path break.
confirmedFrameworkBugs.evidence: packages/tenant/src/Rules/ScopedUnique.php docblock (lines 31-34) promises: 'When the configured tenant FK column does not exist on the target table, the tenant filter is skipped (resilient to misconfiguration).' But validate() (lines 70-80) unconditionally applies `$query->where($tenantKey, $tenant->getKey())` whenever a tenant is current and `$tenantKey !== null` (default `tenant_id` from resolveDefaultTenantKey()). There is NO hasColumn/getColumnListing check anywhere (grep confirms 'column does not exist' appears only in the comment). `$query->exists()` runs with no try/catch, so using `new ScopedUnique('users','email')` on a non-tenant-scoped table while any tenant is active produces `SQLSTATE ... column 'tenant_id' not found` instead of the documented graceful global-unique fallback. The resilience scenario is untested: packages/tenant/tests/Unit/ScopedUniqueTest.php has no missing-column case.

appMisuse.title: FileField upload validation emits mimetypes: from acceptedFileTypes(extensions) which always rejects extension-style values
appMisuse.reasoning: Could not reproduce a failure under documented usage. The code at FieldUploadController.php:115 and FileField::getDefaultRules (FileField.php:170) do build `mimetypes:` from getAcceptedFileTypes(), but acceptedFileTypes() is unambiguously contracted to take MIME types, not extensions: the method signature is `acceptedFileTypes(array $mimeTypes)` with `@param array<int,string> $mimeTypes` (FileField.php:84-86); EVERY planning-spec example uses real MIME types (PLANNING/05-api-php.md:371 `['image/jpeg','image/png']`, :421 `['text/csv']`); EVERY test passes real MIME types (FileFieldTest.php, FieldSerializationSnapshotTest.php: `['application/pdf','image/png']` etc.); and ImageField's defaults are real MIME types `['image/jpeg','image/png','image/webp']` (ImageField.php:22). Laravel's `mimetypes:` rule is the CORRECT rule to validate MIME-type values, so a documented call (e.g. `->acceptedFileTypes(['application/pdf'])`) validates real PDF uploads successfully. The only failure path requires the app to pass extension strings like `['pdf','docx']`, which violates the documented, exemplified, and tested contract — i.e. app-misuse, not a framework defect. The reported 'internal inconsistency' where the upload controller re-adds `mimetypes:` for ImageField (while ImageField::getDefaultRules drops them in favour of `image`) is cosmetically redundant but functionally harmless: image/jpeg|png|webp uploads pass both the `image` rule and the MIME `mimetypes:` rule, so no spurious rejection occurs. No showcase coverage and no reproduction of a real-data failure under documented usage. This is a known footgun (HTML accept attr permits extensions, so a careless user could pass them) but does not rise to a reproduced framework bug; default isReal=false stands.

notBugs.title: NavigationItem::resource() generates resource URL without the /admin panel prefix → sidebar links 404
notBugs.reasoning: The code line is accurately quoted: NavigationItem::resource() (packages/nav/src/NavigationItem.php:90) emits '/'.ltrim($slug,'/') = '/{slug}', with no panel prefix, while resource routes are mounted under Route::prefix($path) (default 'admin') in ArqelServiceProvider, so the canonical index URL is '/admin/{slug}'. That internal inconsistency is genuine. HOWEVER the reported impact — 'sidebar links 404' in the baseline app — is NOT reproducible. The Sidebar (packages-js/ui/src/shell/Sidebar.tsx) sources items from useNavigation() -> the Inertia 'panel.navigation' shared prop. That prop is produced by HandleArqelInertiaRequests::buildNavigation() (line 230: rtrim($panelPath,'/').'/'.$slug), which correctly prepends the panel path, yielding '/admin/{slug}'. A full-repo grep shows Arqel\\Nav\\Navigation and NavigationItem::resource() are never instantiated by core, by apps/showcase, or anywhere on the Sidebar data path (the only non-package reference is a Composer require string in SetupScriptGenerator; the showcase only has it in vendor autoload, unused). So arqel-dev/nav is a standalone, unwired builder whose '/{slug}' output never reaches the rendered sidebar. No sidebar link 404s in apps/showcase on main; the 'Net effect' in the evidence is false. Additionally the docblock references Resource::getIndexUrl() which does not exist. Classified not-a-bug because there is no reproducible application-facing defect — it is a latent inconsistency in dead/parallel code (severity low at most), and could only manifest if someone manually wired arqel-dev/nav into a custom sidebar, which the baseline does not do.
